6-0.v: What Is an Accounting Information System (AIS)?

An AIS collects, stores, and processes financial and accounting data to provide information to decision makers. The AIS supports ALL business transactions through interconnected TRANSACTION CYCLES — the full flow from business event to financial statement.

r: Sales order, shipping document, invoice, remittance advice
Purchase requisition, purchase order, receiving report, vendor invoice
BOM, materials requisition, job cost sheet, production report
Loan agreements, stock certificates, amortisation schedules

v: Trigger: Customer places order (sales order created)
Credit check: Creditworthiness verified before accepting the order
Picking & Shipping: Warehouse picks goods; shipping document created
Billing: Invoice sent to customer (accounts receivable created)
Collection: Customer pays; cash applied to AR
Key controls: Credit approval, segregation of billing/collection, lockbox systems, reconcile AR to GL
Trigger: Department identifies need → purchase requisition created
Purchase Order (PO): Purchasing department issues PO to approved vendor
Receiving: Goods received; Receiving Report created (3-way match: PO + Receiving Report + Vendor Invoice)
Accounts Payable: Invoice recorded; payment approved
Disbursement: Payment issued to vendor
Key controls: 3-way match, segregation of duties (ordering/receiving/paying), competitive bidding, approved vendor list

6-0.v: Purchase Order + Receiving Report + Vendor Invoice must ALL agree before payment is approved

v: Production Planning: Master Production Schedule drives material and capacity planning
Materials Requisition: Raw materials released from storeroom to production (authorised document)
Job Cost Sheet: Records DM, DL, and OH applied to each job or batch
Completion: Finished goods transferred from WIP to Finished Goods inventory
Costing: Standard vs actual costs compared; variances calculated
Key controls: Pre-numbered materials requisitions, access controls on storeroom, periodic physical inventory counts
Hire: Employment authorised by HR — separate from payroll processing
Time Recording: Timesheets or time clocks capture hours worked
Payroll Calculation: Gross pay calculated; deductions applied (taxes, benefits)
Payroll Disbursement: Cheques or direct deposits issued
Payroll Register: Complete record of all payroll transactions
Key controls: HR authorises hires (separate from payroll dept), supervisors approve timesheets, imprest payroll account, limit access to payroll master file
Debt issuance: Board authorises borrowing; loan agreement executed; cash received
Interest accrual and payment: Monthly interest expense recorded; payments made per schedule
Equity issuance: Board authorises share issuance; subscription and collection recorded
Dividend payments: Board declares dividend; payments made to shareholders
Key controls: Board authorisation for all financing, independent confirmation with lenders, reconcile debt schedules to GL

6-0.v: All five cycles feed into the GENERAL LEDGER — the central repository of all accounting entries. The general ledger serves as the basis for financial statement preparation. ERP systems automate this integration, posting transactions from each cycle to the GL in real time.

Exam Tips: 5 cycles — Revenue-to-Cash, Expenditure, Production, HR/Payroll, Financing. 3-Way Match = PO + Receiving Report + Vendor Invoice (MUST match before payment). Key control: SEPARATE the functions of ordering, receiving, and paying (SOD). Payroll: HR hires (separate from payroll); supervisors approve time. Production: materials requisition authorises release from storeroom. All cycles feed the General Ledger.

6-1.v: The Role of Technology in Modern Finance

Technology and data analytics have transformed management accounting. Finance professionals are now expected to extract insights from large datasets, use BI tools, understand ERP systems, and evaluate cybersecurity risks. The CMA exam tests these competencies under Section 6.

r: Sales report showing last quarter revenue
Drill-down analysis of why sales dropped
Forecasting next quarter sales using regression

v: Structured Data: Organized in rows/columns (databases, spreadsheets) — easy to analyze
Unstructured Data: No predefined format (emails, social media, videos) — requires special tools
Big Data: High Volume, Velocity, and Variety (the 3 Vs) — too large for traditional databases
Data Warehouse: Central repository of integrated data from multiple sources for analysis and reporting
Data Mining: Discovering patterns and relationships in large datasets using statistical tools

6-1.v: Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems integrate ALL business processes into one system — finance, HR, supply chain, manufacturing, and sales share a SINGLE database. Examples: SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics.

v: Benefits: Real-time data, eliminates data silos, improves accuracy, automates processes
Risks: High implementation cost, data migration challenges, requires extensive training
Internal Control consideration: ERP requires strong IT controls (access controls, audit trails)
Confidentiality: Data accessible only to authorized users
Integrity: Data is accurate and complete, not tampered with
Availability: Systems are accessible when needed (uptime)
Key controls: Access controls, encryption, firewalls, backup procedures, disaster recovery plans
Robotic Process Automation (RPA): Software robots automating repetitive rule-based tasks (e.g., invoice processing, bank reconciliations)
Artificial Intelligence (AI) & Machine Learning: Detecting patterns, anomalies, and fraud in financial data
Blockchain: Distributed ledger providing transparent, tamper-proof transaction records
Cloud Computing: On-demand access to computing resources — reduces IT infrastructure costs

6-1.v: Exam Tips: 4 types of analytics in order: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (complexity increases). Big Data = 3 Vs (Volume, Velocity, Variety). ERP integrates ALL business functions into ONE database. RPA automates REPETITIVE tasks. Blockchain = immutable distributed ledger. CIA Triad for cybersecurity: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.

6-2.v: Data governance is the overall MANAGEMENT of data availability, usability, integrity, and security. It ensures data is accurate, consistent, and trustworthy across the organization — critical for management decision-making.

r: Missing revenue figures in a report
Different totals in HR vs. payroll for same employees
Month-end close takes 15 days instead of 3
Date field contains text instead of a date
Same vendor entered twice with slightly different names

6-2.v: MDM creates a SINGLE AUTHORITATIVE SOURCE ('golden record') for key business data — customers, vendors, products, employees. This eliminates conflicting data across systems and ensures all departments work from the same information.

v: Creation/Capture: Data enters the organization through transactions, forms, sensors
Storage: Databases, data warehouses, data lakes
Processing & Analysis: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes prepare data for analysis
Archiving: Older data moved to lower-cost storage
Deletion/Retention: Data retained per legal/regulatory requirements, then securely deleted
Data Owner: Business executive accountable for data quality in their domain
Data Steward: Operational role — day-to-day management of data quality, definitions, and standards
Data Custodian: IT role — technical management, storage, and security of data
Chief Data Officer (CDO): Enterprise-level leadership of data strategy and governance

6-2.v: Exam Tips: 6 data quality dimensions — Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity, Uniqueness. MDM = single golden record for master data. ETL = Extract, Transform, Load. Data Owner = accountable (business). Data Steward = operational quality management. Poor data quality = garbage in, garbage out.

6-3.v: ERP systems are modular — organizations implement the modules they need. All modules share a SINGLE integrated database, enabling real-time data flow across the enterprise.

r: General ledger, accounts payable, accounts receivable, asset accounting, financial statements
Cost centers, profit centers, internal orders, profitability analysis
Order management, pricing, billing, customer master data
Procurement, inventory management, vendor master, goods receipt
Manufacturing orders, capacity planning, bill of materials
ALL modules go live simultaneously on one date
Highest risk — entire company transitions at once
Modules or business units go live in stages
Old and new systems run simultaneously until new is proven
Safest — but costly to run two systems

v: Benefits: Real-time data, eliminates silos, process automation, improved controls via audit trails and role-based access
Risks: High cost, change management resistance, data migration complexity, customization makes upgrades difficult, over-reliance risk
Segregation of duties configured via role-based access — one person cannot approve AND process payment
Automated three-way match: ERP matches PO, receiving report, and invoice automatically
Audit trails: All transactions logged with user ID and timestamp
Change management controls: Program changes require authorization and testing

6-3.v: Exam Tips: ERP = ONE database for ALL modules. Big bang = highest risk. Parallel running = safest but most expensive. Key benefits: real-time data, no silos, automation. Key risks: cost, change management, data migration. ERP SOD = role-based access. Three-way match automatable in ERP.

6-4.v: Business Intelligence converts RAW DATA into MEANINGFUL INSIGHTS for business decision-making. BI tools help managers monitor performance, identify trends, and make data-driven decisions.

r: Recording day-to-day transactions with speed and integrity
Analyzing large datasets for decision support
Finance analyzing 5 years of sales by region

v: Dashboard: Visual display of KPIs — real-time performance monitoring
Drill-down: Go from summary to detail (total sales → by region → by product → by customer)
Slice and Dice: Analyzing data from different dimensions (time, geography, product line)
Balanced Scorecard: Links KPIs to strategic objectives across four perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth

6-4.v: Exam Tips: Data Warehouse = structured, clean — for BI reporting. Data Lake = raw, unstructured + structured — for data scientists. Data Mart = subset of DW for one department. OLTP = transactions (ERP). OLAP = analysis (BI). Dashboard = visual KPI monitoring. Drill-down = summary to detail. BSC four perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth.

6-5.v: Regression analysis identifies relationships between variables. In management accounting, it is used for COST ESTIMATION — finding the relationship between costs and cost drivers.

r: Total cost = Fixed cost + (Variable rate × Activity)
Total cost driven by multiple cost drivers
0 to 1 — % of variation in Y explained by X
R²=0.85 means 85% of cost variation explained by the driver

v: Trend: Long-term upward or downward movement
Seasonal: Predictable patterns within a year (retail peaks in Q4)
Cyclical: Multi-year economic cycles
Random/Irregular: Unpredictable one-time events
Random: Every item has equal probability — most statistically valid
Systematic: Every nth item selected
Stratified: Population divided into groups; random sample from each — ensures subgroups represented
Cluster: Randomly select entire clusters — efficient for geographically dispersed populations
Null Hypothesis (H₀): No difference or relationship exists
p-value: If p < 0.05, reject H₀ (result statistically significant at 95% confidence)
Type I Error: Rejecting a TRUE null hypothesis (false positive)
Type II Error: Failing to reject a FALSE null hypothesis (false negative)

6-5.v: Exam Tips: Y = a + bX. 'a' = fixed cost. 'b' = variable cost per unit. R² closer to 1 = better fit. Stratified = ensures subgroups represented. Type I = false positive. Type II = false negative. Line chart = trends. Scatter plot = correlation. Time series: Trend + Seasonal + Cyclical + Random.

r: Deceptive emails tricking users into revealing credentials or authorizing wire transfers
Malware that encrypts files and demands ransom for the key
Manipulating PEOPLE into divulging confidential information
Inserting malicious code into database queries to access unauthorized data
Overwhelming a system with traffic until it crashes

v: Preventive: Firewalls, multi-factor authentication (MFA), encryption, access controls, security awareness training
Detective: Intrusion detection systems (IDS), log monitoring, SIEM, vulnerability scanning
Corrective: Incident response plan, disaster recovery, patch management, system restoration from backups
Symmetric: Same key for encryption AND decryption — fast but key distribution is a challenge
Asymmetric (Public Key): Public key encrypts, private key decrypts — used for HTTPS/TLS
Hash Functions: One-way transformation — verifies data integrity (SHA-256)
NIST Cybersecurity Framework: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
ISO 27001: International standard for information security management systems
SOC 2 Reports: Auditor report on service organization controls — Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy

6-6.v: Exam Tips: Phishing = fake emails to steal credentials. Ransomware = encrypts files, demands payment. MFA = best preventive control. Asymmetric encryption: PUBLIC key encrypts, PRIVATE key decrypts. Hash = one-way, verifies INTEGRITY. NIST CSF: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover. SOC 2 = 5 Trust Services Criteria.

r: Resources shared among multiple organizations — cost-efficient, scalable
Dedicated infrastructure for one organization — most secure, for sensitive data
Mix of public and private — flexibility + security
Shared by organizations with common requirements (e.g., government)

v: Benefits: Converts CapEx to OpEx, scalability, accessibility from anywhere, built-in disaster recovery, automatic updates
Risks: Data security (encryption in transit and at rest required), compliance/GDPR data residency, vendor lock-in, shared responsibility model

6-7.v: Cloud provider secures the INFRASTRUCTURE (physical data centers, hardware, networking). The CUSTOMER secures their DATA, access controls, application configurations, and compliance.

Exam Tips: IaaS = virtual servers (you manage OS up). PaaS = development platform (you manage apps). SaaS = complete software (you manage config only). Cloud converts CapEx to OpEx. Shared responsibility: provider secures infrastructure, customer secures DATA. GDPR = data residency risk. Private cloud = most secure.

6-8.v: Blockchain is a DISTRIBUTED, DECENTRALIZED LEDGER recording transactions across many computers so records CANNOT BE ALTERED retroactively. Each block contains data, a timestamp, and a cryptographic hash of the previous block.

v: Decentralized: No single controlling authority — copies held by many nodes
Immutable: Once recorded, data cannot be changed without altering all subsequent blocks
Transparent: All participants can view the ledger (public blockchains)
Consensus Mechanism: New blocks added only when the network agrees (Proof of Work, Proof of Stake)
Cryptographic Security: Each block linked to the previous via hash — tampering is computationally infeasible

r: Governed by a group of organizations (R3 Corda)

v: Smart Contracts: Self-executing programs that automatically execute terms when conditions are met — no intermediary
Finance applications: Faster cross-border payments, supply chain tracking, trade finance automation, real-time immutable audit trails, eliminating reconciliation between counterparties

6-8.v: Cryptocurrency Accounting — Current US GAAP (ASU 2023-08, Effective 2025)

FASB ASU 2023-08 took effect for fiscal years beginning after December 15, 2024 (i.e., January 2025 for calendar-year companies). This standard fundamentally changed how qualifying crypto assets are measured.

r: Still intangible but measured at FAIR VALUE
Cost less impairment — no write-up when FV recovers
Fair value each reporting period — gains AND losses recognized
Unrealized gains AND losses recognized each period through net income
Fungible crypto traded on active markets with no contractual rights (e.g., Bitcoin, Ether) — non-qualifying crypto still uses old intangible model

v: KEY POINT: Under current GAAP (post-ASU 2023-08), qualifying crypto is measured at FAIR VALUE through net income — both increases AND decreases are recognized each period
Non-qualifying crypto assets (those not fungible or not traded on active markets) continue under old ASC 350 intangible model
Disclosure: entities must disclose crypto asset holdings, cost basis, fair value, and restrictions
This is a SIGNIFICANT change — financial statements of crypto-holding companies now show volatile fair value gains/losses each quarter

6-8.v: Exam Tips: Blockchain = IMMUTABLE, DECENTRALIZED ledger. Public = open (Bitcoin). Private = permissioned (enterprise finance). Smart contracts = self-executing code. Key accounting benefit = eliminates reconciliation. Cross-border payments: faster + cheaper than SWIFT. Consensus = network agreement to add blocks. CRYPTO ACCOUNTING (ASU 2023-08, effective 2025): qualifying crypto = FAIR VALUE through net income each period. Old treatment (intangible, cost, no write-up) was pre-2025. Current GAAP = recognize both unrealized gains AND losses.

r: Learns from LABELED training data — told correct answers
Credit scoring, fraud detection (label: fraud/not fraud)
Learns through TRIAL AND ERROR with rewards/penalties

v: Fraud Detection: ML models analyze transaction attributes in real-time to flag anomalies
Credit Risk Assessment: ML scores applications using hundreds of variables
Financial Forecasting: AI analyzes historical patterns + external data for better forecasts
Accounts Payable Automation: AI reads invoices (OCR + NLP), matches to POs, routes for approval
Audit Analytics: AI reviews 100% of transactions to identify control failures
Natural Language Processing (NLP): Reads contracts, financial filings, news for risk signals
Bias: Models trained on biased historical data produce biased outputs
Black Box Problem: Complex models cannot explain their decisions — regulatory challenge
Data Quality: AI is only as good as its training data — garbage in, garbage out
Overfitting: Model performs well on training data but poorly on new data
Model Drift: As the real world changes, model accuracy degrades — requires retraining

6-9.v: Generative AI (Gen AI) uses large language models (LLMs) to generate text, code, and analysis from prompts. Unlike traditional ML (which predicts or classifies), Gen AI creates new content. By 2025-2026 it is actively deployed across finance functions.

r: Auto-draft variance analysis narratives from structured data — analyst reviews and approves
Human review before any external publication
Extract payment terms, obligations, and risk clauses from thousands of contracts
Verify extracted data — LLMs can miss nuances or hallucinate terms
Draft first versions of financial presentations and summaries
CFO sign-off; material facts must be verified against source data
Scan 10-Ks and 10-Qs for specific disclosures or changes from prior period
Auditor cannot rely solely on AI output — must perform independent procedures
Generate written explanations of forecast assumptions and scenario outcomes
Model assumptions must be documented and reviewed by finance team
Draft audit evidence summaries; flag anomalies in large datasets for auditor review
Auditor remains responsible for professional judgment and conclusions

6-9.v: Gen AI Risks Specific to Finance

v: Hallucination: LLMs confidently state incorrect facts — financial figures or regulatory citations may be fabricated. Always verify AI-generated numbers against source systems
Accountability gap: If AI drafts a financial disclosure with an error, management remains legally accountable (SOX 302/906). AI does not shift legal responsibility

Data privacy: Inputting non-public financial data (unreleased earnings, M&A details) into public LLMs may constitute material non-public information (MNPI) disclosure — use private/enterprise deployments
Model transparency: LLMs cannot explain WHY they produced a specific output — creates challenges for audit trail and regulatory review
Overreliance: Finance professionals may stop critically evaluating outputs — institutional knowledge atrophies

6-9.v: Exam Tips: Supervised = labeled data. Unsupervised = finds patterns (anomaly detection). Reinforcement = trial and error. NLP = reads text (contracts, filings). Black box = cannot explain decisions. Overfitting = good on training data, poor on new data. Model drift = accuracy degrades as world changes. Gen AI (LLMs) = generates text/analysis from prompts — NOT a replacement for judgment. Key Gen AI risks: hallucination (fabricates facts), accountability gap (management still liable), MNPI risk (don't input confidential data into public AI). Always require human review of AI-generated financial content.

6-10.v: RPA uses SOFTWARE ROBOTS to automate REPETITIVE, RULE-BASED, high-volume tasks by mimicking human interactions with digital systems — clicking, reading, typing, copying data — without requiring changes to underlying systems.

v: Accounts Payable: Extracting invoice data, matching to POs, routing for approval, posting to ERP
Accounts Receivable: Sending payment reminders, reconciling payments, applying cash to invoices
Month-End Close: Pulling data from multiple systems, preparing journal entries, generating reports
Bank Reconciliation: Downloading bank statements, matching transactions, flagging exceptions
Financial Reporting: Aggregating data from multiple sources, formatting reports, distributing to stakeholders
Benefits: Works 24/7, eliminates data entry errors, processes in seconds, scales instantly for peak periods, complete audit trail of every bot action
Risks: Bots break when underlying systems change (maintenance burden), security of bot credentials, exception handling requires human escalation, automating flawed processes amplifies errors

6-10.v: RPA handles STRUCTURED, RULE-BASED tasks. AI handles UNSTRUCTURED data and JUDGMENT-BASED tasks. Together (intelligent automation = RPA + AI), they can handle invoice reading (AI extracts unstructured data) + processing (RPA handles rule-based posting).

Exam Tips: RPA = software robots mimicking human UI actions. Best for: repetitive, rule-based, structured, high-volume. NOT suitable for: judgment, exceptions, unstructured data. Key finance uses: AP/AR, bank reconciliation, month-end close. Key risk: bots break when UI changes. RPA works on TOP of existing systems — no API integration needed. Complete audit trails.

r: Servers at the company's own facilities
Full control, high maintenance, capital investment
Company owns hardware at third-party data center
Provider owns and manages hardware; company rents capacity
OpEx model, scalable, provider manages infrastructure

v: Firewall: Filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
VPN (Virtual Private Network): Encrypted tunnel over internet for secure remote access
DMZ: Network segment between internet and internal network — hosts public-facing servers
IDS/IPS: Intrusion Detection/Prevention System — monitors traffic for suspicious activity
Relational Database (RDBMS): Structured tables with relationships — SQL language (Oracle, SQL Server)
NoSQL: Flexible schema for unstructured/semi-structured data (MongoDB, Cassandra)

ACID Properties: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state after each transaction), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (committed transactions permanently saved)

r: Maximum acceptable TIME to restore systems after failure
Maximum acceptable DATA LOSS — how old can restored data be?
Fully operational backup — failover in minutes (highest cost)
Partially configured — hours to activate (moderate cost)
Empty facility — days to activate (lowest cost)

6-11.v: Exam Tips: RTO = time to restore. RPO = acceptable data loss. Hot site = fastest (highest cost). Cold site = slowest (lowest cost). Firewall = network traffic filter. VPN = encrypted remote access. ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Relational DB = structured, SQL. NoSQL = unstructured/flexible.

r: System architecture, security design, UI design
Build security and controls INTO the design
Deploy to production, data migration, training
Linear, sequential — complete each phase before next
Iterative sprints — working software delivered frequently

v: All changes to production must be AUTHORIZED — no unauthorized modifications
Testing required: Unit test → Integration test → UAT before production
SOD: Programmers do NOT have access to production — separate deployment team
Documentation: What changed, why, who approved, when deployed
Emergency changes: Fast-track process with after-the-fact documentation — still requires authorization
Rollback plan: Ability to reverse changes if problems emerge

6-12.v: DevSecOps — Security Embedded in the CI/CD Pipeline

DevSecOps is the modern evolution of 'security by design' — it operationalizes security by embedding automated security checks DIRECTLY INTO the CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) pipeline, so every code commit is automatically tested for security vulnerabilities without slowing development.

r: SAST — Static Application Security Testing scans source code for vulnerabilities before it builds
Checkmarx, SonarQube detect SQL injection patterns in code
Dependency scanning — checks third-party libraries for known vulnerabilities (CVEs)
OWASP Dependency-Check flags outdated libraries with known exploits
DAST — Dynamic Application Security Testing attacks the running application to find runtime vulnerabilities
OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite test the running app for input validation failures
IaC security scanning checks cloud configuration files for misconfigurations before deployment
Checkov flags AWS S3 buckets configured for public access
Runtime security monitoring — detect anomalies in the live application
RASP (Runtime Application Self-Protection) blocks attacks in real time

v: Key principle: 'Shift left' — move security testing EARLIER in the pipeline (to the left on the timeline), when defects are cheapest to fix

DevSecOps vs Security by Design: Security by Design = an SDLC philosophy (plan security in the design phase). DevSecOps = the OPERATIONAL IMPLEMENTATION using automation — security gates that cannot be bypassed in the deployment pipeline
Finance relevance: DevSecOps is particularly important for finance systems where a vulnerability in a payment API or ERP interface could be exploited to divert funds or exfiltrate customer data
SOX compliance: automated security gates in CI/CD pipelines provide evidence of IT change controls — every commit is tested and the results are logged for audit

6-12.v: Exam Tips: Security controls must be built in during DESIGN (security by design). DevSecOps = operational implementation — automated security gates (SAST, DAST, dependency scanning) in the CI/CD pipeline so every code change is security-tested before deployment. 'Shift left' = test security earlier (cheaper to fix). Programmers must NOT have access to PRODUCTION (SOD). UAT = users confirm system meets requirements. Waterfall = sequential. Agile = iterative sprints. All changes authorized + tested + documented. Emergency changes = still require authorization.

6-13.v: Digital finance transformation integrates DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES into the finance function to fundamentally change how finance teams operate — moving from backward-looking scorekeeping to forward-looking strategic partnership.

v: Continuous Accounting: Automated reconciliations and journal entries throughout the month — eliminate month-end crunch
Integrated Business Planning (IBP): Finance, operations, and HR planning synchronized on a single real-time platform
Finance Shared Services / GBS: Centralizing transactional finance with automation in low-cost centers
Real-Time Reporting: Management dashboards updated continuously

6-13.v: The Evolving Finance Role & Change Management

v: Finance spends less time on data collection (automated) and more time on analysis, business partnering, and strategic advice
New skills required: data literacy, analytics tools, storytelling with data
Biggest barrier to transformation: PEOPLE — resistance to change, lack of skills — not technology
Leadership sponsorship critical: CFO must champion the transformation

6-13.v: Exam Tips: Transformation moves finance from scorekeeping to strategic partnership. Continuous accounting = automated reconciliations throughout the month (not just month-end). IBP = integrated planning across finance, ops, HR. Finance new role = data-driven trusted advisor. Biggest barrier = PEOPLE resistance to change, not technology.

v: 1. IDENTIFY: Catalog all digital assets — classify by sensitivity and criticality
2. ASSESS: Evaluate threats and vulnerabilities. Risk = Likelihood × Impact
3. RESPOND: Implement controls aligned with risk appetite (accept, mitigate, transfer, avoid)
4. MONITOR: Continuous monitoring, vulnerability scanning, penetration testing
5. RECOVER: Incident response plan, communication plan, forensics, restoration

r: IT controls that support ICFR — cybersecurity failures = potential material weakness
Breach notification within 72 hours; data protection requirements
Up to 4% of global annual revenue
Card network fines; loss of card processing
Disclose material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days

v: Vulnerability Assessment: Automated scanning to IDENTIFY known weaknesses — like checking if doors are unlocked
Penetration Testing: Ethical hackers EXPLOIT vulnerabilities — actually try to break in to test real defenses
Bug Bounty Programs: Companies pay external researchers to find and report vulnerabilities
Red Team / Blue Team: Red team attacks; blue team defends — tests detection and response

6-14.v: Exam Tips: Cyber risk = Likelihood × Impact. Cyber insurance = transfers residual risk. GDPR breach notification = 72 hours. SEC rules = material incident disclosure in 4 business days. SOX 404 = IT controls part of ICFR. PCI DSS = credit card data. Pen test = ethical hackers EXPLOIT. Vulnerability assessment = identifies weaknesses only.

r: Lawful basis, data subject rights, 72-hr breach notification, data minimization
Right to know, right to delete, right to opt-out of data sale
Privacy notices, safeguards rule — protect non-public personal financial information
Card network fines; loss of card processing

v: Right to Access: Request a copy of their personal data
Right to Rectification: Correct inaccurate data
Right to Erasure ('Right to be Forgotten'): Request deletion — with conditions
Right to Data Portability: Receive data in machine-readable format
Right to Object: Object to processing for direct marketing
Right to Restriction: Limit processing while a dispute is resolved
Privacy by Design: Build privacy protections INTO systems and processes from the start — mandated by GDPR Art. 25
Data Minimization: Collect only what you need for the stated purpose
Storage Limitation: Keep data only as long as necessary
Financial records retention: SEC requires 7 years (public companies); IRS requires 3-7 years for tax records
Secure deletion: Physical destruction or cryptographic erasure

6-15.v: Exam Tips: GDPR = EU. Right to be Forgotten = Right to Erasure. CCPA = California. GLBA = US financial institutions. PCI DSS = 12 requirements, credit card data. Privacy by Design = build privacy in from the START. Data minimization = collect only what is needed. Financial records = 7 years (SEC). GDPR max fine = 4% global revenue.

v: IoT = physical devices (sensors, RFID tags) transmitting real-time data
Finance applications: real-time inventory tracking (RFID), asset tracking, usage-based billing, predictive maintenance cost planning
Accounting impact: near-real-time inventory valuation; continuous asset monitoring
Risk: IoT devices expand the cybersecurity attack surface — each device is a potential entry point
Digital twin = a virtual REAL-TIME REPLICA of a physical asset, process, or system updated from sensor data
Finance applications: simulate financial impact of operational changes BEFORE implementation — test 'what-if' scenarios
Manufacturing: predict equipment failures — reduce maintenance costs and unplanned downtime
Uses quantum mechanics (superposition, entanglement) to solve complex problems exponentially faster
Finance potential: portfolio optimization, risk modeling, fraud detection
Critical risk: Quantum could BREAK current encryption — 'Q-Day' preparation requires migrating to quantum-resistant algorithms (NIST post-quantum cryptography)
5G: ultra-fast wireless connectivity enabling real-time data from IoT devices and remote locations
Edge Computing: data processed CLOSE TO WHERE IT IS GENERATED (at the device) — reduces latency and bandwidth vs. sending everything to a central cloud
Finance impact: real-time transaction processing at remote locations, faster fraud detection

6-16.v: Innovation Trigger → Peak of Inflated Expectations → Trough of Disillusionment → Slope of Enlightenment → Plateau of Productivity. Finance teams should be cautious of technologies at the Peak — wait for the Plateau before major investment.

Exam Tips: IoT = physical devices + real-time data. RFID = asset/inventory tracking. Digital twin = virtual replica updated in real-time. Quantum = breaks current encryption (Q-Day). Prepare with post-quantum cryptography. Edge computing = process data AT THE SOURCE, reduces latency. 5G enables real-time IoT. Hype Cycle: Peak → Trough → Plateau.

v: Reliability: Do IT systems produce accurate, complete financial data?
Security: Are systems and data protected from unauthorized access?
Integrity: Is data processed completely and accurately?
Availability: Are systems accessible when needed?
Compliance: Do IT systems comply with SOX, GDPR, and other regulations?

r: Controls relevant to USER ENTITIES' financial reporting (ICFR)
External auditors of companies using the service
Controls over Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy (5 Trust Services Criteria)
Same as SOC 2 but PUBLIC — general use

v: Generalized Audit Software (GAS): IDEA, ACL/Galvanize — analyzes 100% of transactions to find anomalies, duplicates, gaps in sequences
Test Data: Input specially designed test transactions to verify controls respond correctly
Parallel Simulation: Re-process actual transactions using auditor's own program — compare to system output
Embedded Audit Modules: Audit code built INTO the production system — monitors transactions continuously

6-17.v: Exam Tips: SOC 1 = financial reporting controls. SOC 2 = 5 Trust Services (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy). SOC 3 = public version of SOC 2. GAS (IDEA, ACL) = analyzes 100% of data. Test data = tests controls. Embedded audit module = continuous monitoring. IT audit evaluates: reliability, security, integrity, availability, compliance.

r: IT governance — aligns IT with business goals; 40 objectives across governance and management (ISACA)
IT service management best practices — Service Strategy, Design, Transition, Operation, Improvement
International standard for IT governance by governing bodies — six principles
Enterprise architecture framework — aligning IT and business architecture

v: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO): ALL costs over technology's life — purchase, implementation, training, maintenance, support, replacement
ROI: (Benefits − Costs) / Costs × 100%. Benefits include cost savings, productivity gains, risk reduction
Payback Period: Time to recover the initial investment from net benefits
NPV: Present value of all future benefits minus costs — preferred for multi-year investments (accounts for time value of money)

r: Unique competitive requirement not available off-shelf
Perfect fit vs. high cost and long timeline
Lower cost, faster implementation vs. may not fit perfectly
OpEx model, scalable vs. data security and customization limits

v: Sustaining Innovation: Improving existing products/processes — incremental, lower risk
Disruptive Innovation (Christensen): New technology that transforms an industry — initially inferior but eventually displaces incumbents (cloud vs. on-premise ERP)
MVP (Minimum Viable Product): Launch basic version quickly, get user feedback, iterate — reduces wasted development effort

6-18.v: Exam Tips: COBIT = IT governance (aligns IT with business). ITIL = IT service management. TCO = total cost over life (not just purchase). NPV preferred for multi-year investments. Build = custom, highest cost. Buy = off-shelf, standard. SaaS = lowest maintenance, OpEx. Disruptive innovation = initially inferior, eventually displaces. MVP = launch fast, iterate. Innovation portfolio: 70% sustaining, 20% adjacent, 10% transformational.
